md: Probability problems often boil down to **counting**: how many total outcomes are there, and how many are favorable? When counting by hand isn't practical, two simple principles do most of the heavy lifting.
## 1. The Multiplication (Fundamental Counting) Principle
If a task is done in **stages**, multiply the choices at each stage.
**Example.** A menu has 4 appetizers, 6 entrees, 3 desserts. How many possible 3-course meals?
$4 \cdot 6 \cdot 3 = 72$ meals.
**Example.** How many 4-letter codes can be made from the 26 letters (repetition allowed)?

blocks.title: When to multiply
blocks.body: Use multiplication when each step is **independent** — what you choose at one step doesn't restrict the next step's options. (Or you've already accounted for restrictions.)

md: ## 2. Multiplication With Restrictions
**Example.** How many 4-digit codes if no digit can repeat?
First digit: 10 choices. Second: only 9 left. Third: 8 left. Fourth: 7 left.
$10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 = 5040$ codes.
**Example.** How many ways to arrange 5 books on a shelf?
First spot: 5 choices. Second: 4 left. Third: 3. Fourth: 2. Fifth: 1.
$5! = 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 120$. (This is the 'permutation' count, see next leaf.)
## 3. The Addition Principle
If a task is done in *one of several disjoint ways*, **add** the choices.
**Example.** You can drive to work or take the bus. There are 3 driving routes and 2 bus routes. How many ways to get to work?
(Multiplying ($3 \cdot 2 = 6$) would be wrong — you can't drive AND take the bus at the same time.)

blocks.title: Multiplication vs addition — when to use which
blocks.body: **Multiplication** ($\times$): combining choices in **stages** (do step 1 AND step 2 AND ...). **Addition** ($+$): choosing from **alternatives** (do option A OR option B OR ...). The 'and' vs 'or' test usually decides.

md: ## 4. Tree Diagrams
A **tree diagram** branches out at each stage, showing all paths.
**Example.** A coin flip followed by a die roll. The tree has 2 branches (H, T) at first, each splitting into 6 (1–6) — total $2 \cdot 6 = 12$ leaves.
**When to draw one:** small problems, when you want to enumerate every outcome, or when stages have different numbers of choices depending on what came before.
### Worked example — license plates
A license plate has 6 characters total: 3 letters from A–Z followed by 3 digits 0–9, with repetition allowed.
**Letters (3 positions, 26 choices each):** $26 \cdot 26 \cdot 26 = 17{,}576$.
**Digits (3 positions, 10 choices each):** $10 \cdot 10 \cdot 10 = 1000$.
### Worked example — passwords
How many 8-character passwords from lowercase letters and digits (36 characters), no repetition allowed?
$36 \cdot 35 \cdot 34 \cdot 33 \cdot 32 \cdot 31 \cdot 30 \cdot 29 \approx 1.22 \cdot 10^{12}$.
Way more than enough for any practical purpose.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Multiplication principle:** combining stages → multiply. **Addition principle:** choosing alternatives → add. **'And'** typically means multiply; **'or'** typically means add. Tree diagrams visualize when stages have varying choice counts.
